role_summary: Chair of the Cambridge (Maryland) Nonviolent Action Committee, 1961-1964; rejected the 1963 Treaty of Cambridge that the Kennedy administration brokered; SNCC executive-board member.
ai_reading: Richardson's axis positions are all small: -0.15 on nonviolence-self-defense, +0.06 on sacred-secular, +0.03 on tactical-strategic, -0.20 on southern-northern, +0.11 on individual-collective. None exceed +/-0.20 in absolute value, placing her among the most centrist voices in the entire 136-interview corpus. The cultural placement of Richardson, anchored by her on-the-record refusal to endorse the 1963 Treaty of Cambridge and her statement that "a first-class citizen does not beg for freedom," would have predicted a sharper position on the nonviolence vs self-defense axis or on the individual conscience vs collective discipline axis; instead the embedding reads her testimony as quiet across all five dimensions. Her top semantic neighbors are Reginald Robinson (entry 101, count 104), William Lamar Strickland (entry 127), Judy Richardson (entry 69), Junius Williams (entry 72), and Ekwueme Michael Thelwell (entry 34); the cluster mixes SNCC, post-SNCC Black-Power figures, and the Maryland-and-Mid-Atlantic node where her Cambridge movement organized, broader than the strict SNCC-executive-board placement her cultural label carries.
biographical_paragraph: Gloria Hayes Richardson led the Cambridge Nonviolent Action Committee through one of the most-protracted civil rights confrontations of the early 1960s, the Cambridge, Maryland movement that produced the 1963 Treaty of Cambridge, the Kennedy-administration-brokered settlement she declined to endorse [src: 2, 5]. She was born Gloria St. Clair Hayes in Baltimore on May 6, 1922, and her family moved to Cambridge when she was six, so she grew up in the Eastern Shore town her grandfather had served on the city council [src: 5, 8]. She sat on SNCC's executive board after attending the organization's 1962 Atlanta conference [src: 2]. The Cambridge campaign reached her family in late 1961, when SNCC field workers arrived on the Eastern Shore and her cousin Frederick St. Clair and his father posted bond for the protesters [src: 1, 4]. In 1964 she stood against the citizens-council visit of Alabama Governor George Wallace; demonstrators that spring faced Maryland National Guard troops and tear gas [src: 1, 4]. Her on-the-record line, that "a first-class citizen does not beg for freedom," framed her position that access to public accommodations should not be put to a referendum [src: 2]. After Cambridge she married photographer Frank Dandridge, her second husband, and moved to New York, where she continued organizing work; she died on July 15, 2021, at ninety-nine [src: 2, 5]. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 49 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked about the referendum on public accommodations that the negotiated settlement would have required, Richardson described why she rejected putting the question to a vote:
interview_snippets.quote: Well, we didn't ever think we were supposed to vote on whether we could go into a restaurant.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
interview_snippets.lead_in: Describing how organizing roles in Cambridge fell out between the men who worked daytime jobs and the women who ran the movement's daily business:
interview_snippets.quote: So it was almost a divisional labor. Men went to work if something happened, the women could shoot, too.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
interview_snippets.lead_in: On how she handled the fear during the months of armed confrontation, paraphrasing a line from the poet Claude McKay that she would repeat to herself:
interview_snippets.quote: If I'm pinned against the wall like a hole, let me not die like one.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge)
interview_snippets.lead_in: Reflecting on negotiating with national figures from the Kennedy administration to the NAACP without deference to their titles:
interview_snippets.quote: So, I really did not have a kind of awe of what are normally important people.

interview_snippets.speaker: Judy Richardson
interview_snippets.lead_in: SNCC worker Judy Richardson, who traveled to Cambridge from the SNCC office, recalled Gloria Richardson's stance on nonviolence:
interview_snippets.quote: One of the things that was clear with Gloria, and we can get into this later, but I want to, well, I'm thinking about it, was that she was never philosophically nonviolent.

interview_snippets.speaker: Reginald Robinson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Reginald Robinson, the SNCC field secretary assigned to Cambridge, recalled how Richardson answered the white establishment's proposed referendum on public accommodations:
interview_snippets.quote: Gloria said that no black folks was going to vote for the referendum.

movement_context: The Cambridge Nonviolent Action Committee (CNAC) Richardson co-founded in 1962 was the SNCC-affiliated organizing body on Maryland's Eastern Shore, the Mid-Atlantic node of SNCC's project geography that the Carson 1981 institutional history of SNCC documents as separate from the deep-South Mississippi and Alabama project counties [src: 3]. CNAC's two-front campaign against employment discrimination (the segregated hiring practices at Cambridge's Phillips Packing Company and downtown commercial establishments) and housing discrimination (the redlined Second Ward where the town's Black residents were confined) escalated through the spring of 1963 from sit-ins at the Dorchester County Courthouse into a city-wide nonviolent campaign that, by Peter Levy's 2003 University Press of Florida history of the movement, was the longest-running sustained civil-rights confrontation in the Mid-Atlantic in the early-1960s period [src: 4]. The June 11, 1963 declaration of martial law in Cambridge by Maryland Governor J. Millard Tawes followed a CNAC march on the Dorchester County Courthouse the night of June 10 that white counter-protesters attacked; the Tawes proclamation activated the Maryland National Guard, which occupied Cambridge in continuous formation from July 12, 1963 through July 1964 (the longest peacetime National Guard occupation of an American city since Reconstruction) [src: 4]. The photograph of Richardson pushing aside a National Guard bayonet outside the Dizzyland restaurant on July 21, 1963 (taken by Fred Ward for the Black Star photo agency) became the iconic visual document of the Cambridge confrontation and is held in the Maryland Center for History and Culture's collections [src: 4, 5]. The July 23, 1963 Treaty of Cambridge negotiated at the Justice Department by U.S. Attorney General Robert F. Kennedy with Richardson, CNAC, Mayor Calvin Mowbray, and Governor Tawes contained five terms including desegregation of public facilities, public-school integration, public-housing construction in the Second Ward, a biracial human-relations commission, and a charter amendment requiring a public referendum to enact the desegregation provisions; Richardson's refusal to endorse the referendum requirement, on the position that civil rights should not be contingent on a majority vote, is the institutional record's clearest example of a Movement leader rejecting a Kennedy-administration-brokered settlement [src: 4, 5]. At the August 28, 1963 March on Washington, Richardson was one of only six women named in the Tribute to Negro Women Fighters for Freedom that A. Philip Randolph and Daisy Bates anchored on the platform program (alongside Bates, Diane Nash Bevel, Mahalia Jackson, Rosa Parks, and Myrlie Evers); the institutional framing of the March's program by Bayard Rustin and Randolph's planning committee acknowledged the women's organizing in the form of the Tribute rather than as primary speakers [src: 4, 6].
legacy_and_reception: Richardson married Look magazine and Time photographer Frank Dandridge in 1964 and moved to New York City, where she worked for HARYOU-ACT and the National Council for Negro Women on community-development and youth-employment programs through the late 1960s and 1970s; the institutional record of her post-Cambridge organizing is documented in Joseph R. Fitzgerald's 2017 University Press of Kentucky biography The Struggle Is Eternal: Gloria Richardson and Black Liberation, the standard scholarly volume on her continuing public-life record after the Cambridge years [src: 5]. The Fitzgerald biography's title quotes a phrase Richardson used in her own correspondence to frame Black liberation as a continuing institutional project rather than the discrete-victory framing the Movement's mainstream narrative had often imposed; the volume draws on Richardson's personal papers, the CNAC institutional records held at the Maryland Center for History and Culture, and Annette Brock's 1979 Howard University doctoral dissertation Cambridge, Maryland: The Black Freedom Fight, 1961-1965, the underlying primary-source academic work on CNAC [src: 5, 7]. Her death in New York City on July 15, 2021 at ninety-nine drew obituary coverage in the New York Times, Washington Post, and Baltimore Sun that returned the Cambridge Movement to the front of the institutional civil-rights memory after a half-century of relative scholarly neglect; the Maryland State Archives Hall of Records Commission designated her papers as a primary-source collection of state-historical significance, and the Maryland Center for History and Culture's Cambridge Movement holdings remain the principal archival institutional record of CNAC's organizing [src: 5].

gallery.alt: U.S. Attorney General Robert F. Kennedy speaking to a crowd outside the Justice Department, June 14, 1963
gallery.caption: Attorney General Robert F. Kennedy speaking through a megaphone to a crowd of civil rights demonstrators outside the U.S. Department of Justice on June 14, 1963, with a Congress of Racial Equality sign prominently displayed. Six weeks after this photograph, on July 23, 1963, Kennedy convened Richardson, the Cambridge Nonviolent Action Committee, Maryland Governor J. Millard Tawes, and Cambridge Mayor Calvin Mowbray at the Justice Department to negotiate the Treaty of Cambridge that ended the formal phase of the Cambridge campaign; Richardson refused to endorse the Treaty's referendum-requirement provision. Photograph by Warren K. Leffler for U.S. News and World Report.
gallery.repository: Library of Congress, U.S. News and World Report Magazine Collection (Digital ID ppmsca.04295)
gallery.license: No known copyright restrictions (donated to LoC by U.S. News and World Report)

gallery.alt: Maryland Governor J. Millard Tawes at a White House Poultry Tariff Meeting, June 4, 1962
gallery.caption: Maryland Governor J. Millard Tawes at the White House Poultry Tariff Meeting on June 4, 1962. One year after this photograph, Tawes proclaimed martial law in Cambridge, Maryland (June 11, 1963) following Cambridge Nonviolent Action Committee marches on the Dorchester County Courthouse; the Maryland National Guard then occupied Cambridge in continuous formation from July 12, 1963 through July 1964, the longest peacetime National Guard occupation of an American city since Reconstruction. Photograph by Abbie Rowe (White House photographer), held by the John F. Kennedy Presidential Library and Museum (JFKWHP-KN-C22152).
gallery.repository: John F. Kennedy Presidential Library and Museum, Boston (via Wikimedia Commons)

gallery.alt: SNCC poster 'Is He Protecting You?' (1963), featuring a Mississippi Highway Patrol officer photographed by Danny Lyon
gallery.caption: Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) poster 'Is He Protecting You?' from 1963, depicting a Mississippi Highway Patrol officer with arms crossed in a photograph by Danny Lyon. The visual rhetoric of the SNCC poster series questioning the protective function of state-deployed armed force runs in direct thematic parallel to the institutional confrontation Richardson and CNAC faced during the July 1963 through July 1964 Maryland National Guard occupation of Cambridge. Held by the Seattle Public Library and contributed to the Digital Public Library of America.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Seattle Public Library and the Digital Public Library of America)
gallery.license: No Known Copyright (RightsStatements.org designation; the holding institution reasonably believes no copyright or related rights restrict use)

sources.title: Gloria Hayes Richardson (Dandridge) oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Clayborne Carson, In Struggle: SNCC and the Black Awakening of the 1960s
sources.publisher: Harvard University Press, 1981 (Bancroft Prize 1982); WorldCat OCLC 3025953 (the standard institutional history of SNCC including the project-county geography that placed CNAC at the Mid-Atlantic node)

sources.title: Peter B. Levy, Civil War on Race Street: The Civil Rights Movement in Cambridge, Maryland
sources.publisher: University Press of Florida, 2003; WorldCat OCLC 51163879 (the standard scholarly institutional history of the Cambridge Movement, the Tawes martial-law declaration, the Maryland National Guard occupation, and the Treaty of Cambridge negotiation)

sources.title: Joseph R. Fitzgerald, The Struggle Is Eternal: Gloria Richardson and Black Liberation
sources.publisher: University Press of Kentucky, 2017; WorldCat OCLC 971525221 (the standard scholarly biography of Richardson covering the Cambridge years, the marriage to Frank Dandridge, the New York organizing work through HARYOU-ACT and the National Council for Negro Women, and the continuing public-life record)

sources.title: Annette K. Brock, Cambridge, Maryland: The Black Freedom Fight, 1961-1965
sources.publisher: Howard University doctoral dissertation, 1979; WorldCat OCLC 6648186 (the underlying primary-source doctoral research on CNAC's organizing record, drawing on the Maryland Center for History and Culture's Cambridge Movement archival holdings)

sources.title: Faith S. Holsaert, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, Judy Richardson, Betty Garman Robinson, Jean Smith Young, Dorothy M. Zellner, editors, Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC
sources.publisher: University of Illinois Press, 2010; WorldCat OCLC 863206155 (the institutional memoir-anthology of SNCC's women organizers, with a Cambridge Movement chapter by Reginald Robinson and others documenting the CNAC-SNCC working relationship)

sources.title: Howell Raines, My Soul Is Rested: Movement Days in the Deep South Remembered
sources.publisher: Putnam, 1977 (reprinted Penguin, 1983); WorldCat OCLC 3025950 (oral-history interviews with Cambridge Movement participants conducted in the mid-1970s, including Richardson herself, providing the early contemporaneous documentary record of CNAC's organizing)

sources.title: Gloria Richardson Dandridge, Maryland Women's Hall of Fame (1922-2021)
sources.publisher: Maryland State Archives (biographical record establishing her birth in Baltimore as Gloria St. Clair Hayes, the family's move to Cambridge when she was six, her selection to lead CNAC, the July 1963 Treaty of Cambridge negotiation with Robert Kennedy, and her marriage to her second husband, photographer Frank Dandridge)
